turns.content: I'm trying to build a config parser for my weekend project and I'm stuck on implementing the custom hash for maps. My current implementation of the struct uses the standard HashMap but I need to provide my own impl Hash because the default doesn't work with my Eq variant. I've written code that derives Eq correctly, but when I try to use it in a collection like an index map or as a key in another map, the compiler complains about missing the trait bound for Hash. Can someone explain why Hash and Eq must agree here?

The issue is that Rust's type system enforces a strict contract between Eq and Hash; you cannot derive one without deriving the other because they are linked in the trait definition. When you implement custom logic for Eq, such as handling special cases like NaN comparisons or zero values, you must also manually implement impl Hash to ensure consistency. For example, if your Eq considers two floats equal when both are NaN, your Hash implementation must produce identical hashes for those same inputs. If you only derive Eq but not Hash, the compiler will reject code that tries to use your type as a map key because the trait bounds won't match.

That makes sense about the contract, but I'm still confused about how to actually write this for my float-based config values. I tried using serde_json::Value serialization as a temporary hash source, but that feels wrong and adds unnecessary overhead. How do I correctly implement impl Hash manually without relying on external crates or serialization tricks? Is there a specific pattern for hashing custom types like Option<T>?

You should avoid relying on serde_json::Value serialization for performance-critical hash implementations because it introduces unnecessary allocations and dependencies. Instead, implement impl Hash by combining the hashes of each field in your struct using a simple accumulation method like fold or a linear combination. For Option<T>, you can check if the value is None or Some; if it's Some, hash the inner type T, otherwise hash the variant itself. Make sure your manual impl Hash matches the logic in your Eq implementation exactly so that equal inputs always produce equal hashes.
